TEXT.citation: Early, R. J., & Whitehorn, J. (2003). One hundred Paiwan texts. Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University.
TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @book{100paiwantexts, author = {Robert Early and John Whitehorn}, title = {One Hundred Paiwan Texts}, year = {2003}, publisher = {Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University}}
TEXT.source: ti ḍail kati gagang (Kuɫaɫau village, p.224)

S.TRANSL: There were a monkey and a crab.

S.FORM: "ari, kiitjui," aya; sa vaik a maḍusa.
S.PHON: "ari, kiicui," aja; sa vaik a maɖusa.
S.FORM: "ari, kiitjui," aya; sa vaik a madrusa.
S.PHON: "ari, kiiʦui," aja; sa vaik a maɖusa.
S.TRANSL: “Come on, let’s collect loquat fruit,” they said; and the two of them went off.
They got to the tree,
and the crab was going to try to climb up.
But he couldn’t; he just slipped down.

S.FORM: saka ti ḍaiḍail anga a ɫevavaw.
S.PHON: saka ti ɖaiɖaiɭ aŋa a ʎəvavaw.
S.FORM: saka ti draidrail anga a ljevavaw.
S.PHON: saka ti ɖaiɖaiɭ aŋa a ʎəvavaw.
S.TRANSL: So it was the monkey who climbed up.

S.FORM: manu nu izuanga kiniitju ni ḍaiḍail, gesgesen a kaikapu, sa katjani a namalum, sa ɫuɫu-i ni gagang.
S.PHON: manu nu izuaŋa kiniicu ni ɖaiɖaiɭ, gəsgəsən a kaikapu, sa kacani a namaɭum, sa ʎuʎu-i ni gagaŋ.
S.FORM: manu nu izuanga kiniitju ni draidrail, gesgesen a kaikapu, sa katjani a namalum, sa ljulju-i ni gagang.
S.PHON: manu nu izuaŋa kiniiʦu ni ɖaiɖaiɭ, gəsgəsən a kaikapu, sa kaʦani a namaɭum, sa ʎuʎu-i ni gagaŋ.
S.TRANSL: When the monkey had picked any loquats, the crab shook the cloth container, and ripe ones fell out, and the crab picked them up.

S.FORM: "aku a keḍi anga ku kiniitju, qalian? lingu su getselen a ku kaikapu."
S.PHON: "aku a kəɖi aŋa ku kiniicu, qaɭianʔ ɭiŋu su gəʦəɭən a ku kaikapu."
S.FORM: "aku a kedri anga ku kiniitju, qalian' lingu su gecelen a ku kaikapu."
S.PHON: "aku a kəɖi aŋa ku kiniiʦu, qaɭianʔ ɭiŋu su gəʦəɭən a ku kaikapu."
S.TRANSL: “Friend, why are there so few left of what I’ve picked? Perhaps you have torn the cloth.”

S.FORM: "anema naya, qalian? qayamu a su kaikapu, tu ki zua verits kemuda."
S.PHON: "anəma naja, qaɭianʔ qajamu a su kaikapu, tu ki zua vəriʦ kəmuda."
S.FORM: "anema naya, qalian' qayamu a su kaikapu, tu ki zua veric kemuda."
S.PHON: "anəma naja, qaɭianʔ qajamu a su kaikapu, tu ki zua vəriʦ kəmuda."
S.TRANSL: “What are you saying, friend? Look at your cloth, to see whether there is a tear or anything.”
When he looked, there was no tear.

S.FORM: "pai, qalian, ku ɫinuɫu sakamaya," aya ti gagang.
S.PHON: "pai, qaɭian, ku ʎinuʎu sakamaja," aja ti gagaŋ.
S.FORM: "pai, qalian, ku ljinulju sakamaya," aya ti gagang.
S.PHON: "pai, qaɭian, ku ʎinuʎu sakamaja," aja ti gagaŋ.
S.TRANSL: “Well, friend, I just picked them up,” said the crab.
“Let’s go home.”
Next day they went to get miscanthus grass for torches.
“Well, friend, let’s try burning one another.”

S.FORM: "tiaken-ay anan tjaisangas," sa ɫamain ni ḍaiḍail.
S.PHON: "tiakən-aj anan caisaŋas," sa ʎamain ni ɖaiɖaiɭ.
S.FORM: "tiaken-ay anan tjaisangas," sa ljamain ni draidrail.
S.PHON: "tiakən-aj anan ʦaisaŋas," sa ʎamain ni ɖaiɖaiɭ.
S.TRANSL: “Well I’ll go first,” (said the crab), and the monkey set fire (to the grass).
“What do you think of my fire, friend?”
“It’s nothing to a tough male like me.
I’m still here.

S.FORM: sa tisun-ay anan, qalian i ḍaiḍail."
S.PHON: sa tisun-aj anan, qaɭian i ɖaiɖaiɭ."
S.FORM: sa tisun-ay anan, qalian i draidrail."
S.PHON: sa tisun-aj anan, qaɭian i ɖaiɖaiɭ."
S.TRANSL: Now it’s your turn, friend monkey.”

S.FORM: ɫevavaw ta kasiw ti ḍaiḍail; ɫamain ni gagang.
S.PHON: ʎəvavaw ta kasiw ti ɖaiɖaiɭ; ʎamain ni gagaŋ.
S.FORM: ljevavaw ta kasiw ti draidrail; ljamain ni gagang.
S.PHON: ʎəvavaw ta kasiw ti ɖaiɖaiɭ; ʎamain ni gagaŋ.
S.TRANSL: The monkey went up a tree, and the crab set fire to it (or the grass).
“What do you think of my fire, friend?”
“It’s nothing to a tough male like me.
I’m still here.”
Then the fire went out.
The monkey couldn’t be heard any more.

S.FORM: "ku kimaw anan ti ku qali," aya.
S.PHON: "ku kimaw anan ti ku qaɭi," aja.
S.FORM: "ku kimaw anan ti ku qali," aya.
S.PHON: "ku kimaw anan ti ku qaɭi," aja.
S.TRANSL: “I’ll go and look for my friend,” said the crab.
He found him, and he was dead.

S.FORM: "ki ku kuda-i sa ti ku qali, sa mevaɫut?"
S.PHON: "ki ku kuda-i sa ti ku qaɭi, sa məvaʎut"
S.FORM: "ki ku kuda-i sa ti ku qali, sa mevaljut?"
S.PHON: "ki ku kuda-i sa ti ku qaɭi, sa məvaʎut"
S.TRANSL: “What am I to do to bring my friend back to life?”

S.FORM: sa vutsiqi, sa sualapi a qavuvung, sa tsapa-i.
S.PHON: sa vuʦiqi, sa suaɭapi a qavuvuŋ, sa ʦapa-i.
S.FORM: sa vuciqi, sa sualapi a qavuvung, sa capa-i.
S.PHON: sa vuʦiqi, sa suaɭapi a qavuvuŋ, sa ʦapa-i.
S.TRANSL: He cut him open, took out his heart, and dried it.
“How will my friend come back to life?” he said.
Then he came back to life.

S.FORM: "uzai, qalian, a ku kiniḍaluɫan tua se Tjuvaɫuɫukay," ayain.
S.PHON: "uzai, qaɭian, a ku kiniɖaɭuʎan tua sə cuvaʎuʎukaj," ajain.
S.FORM: "uzai, qalian, a ku kinidraluljan tua se Tjuvaljuljukay," ayain.
S.PHON: "uzai, qaɭian, a ku kiniɖaɭuʎan tua sə ʦuvaʎuʎukaj," ajain.
S.TRANSL: “Here, friend, is what I won off the Tjuvaɫuɫukay people,” he said;
and they ate it. And they said: “Come on, let’s go home.”
It got dark.
“Come on, friend, let’s go courting.”

S.FORM: sa "ika ken a uri kisudju, qalian," aya ti gagang.
S.PHON: sa "ika kən a uri kisuɉu, qaɭian," aja ti gagaŋ.
S.FORM: sa "ika ken a uri kisudju, qalian," aya ti gagang.
S.PHON: sa "ika kən a uri kisuɉu, qaɭian," aja ti gagaŋ.
S.TRANSL: “I’m not going courting, friend,” said the crab.

S.FORM: manu kitjaviliɫ ti gagang, sa paka patekuteku tua garaɫigaɫ.
S.PHON: manu kicaviɭiʎ ti gagaŋ, sa paka patəkutəku tua garaʎigaʎ.
S.FORM: manu kitjavililj ti gagang, sa paka patekuteku tua garaljigalj.
S.PHON: manu kiʦaviɭiʎ ti gagaŋ, sa paka patəkutəku tua garaʎigaʎ.
S.TRANSL: But the crab followed, and went along underneath the bed-space.

S.FORM: manu getselen a qalitsi ni ḍaiḍail, sa vaikan.
S.PHON: manu gəʦəɭən a qaɭiʦi ni ɖaiɖaiɭ, sa vaikan.
S.FORM: manu gecelen a qalici ni draidrail, sa vaikan.
S.PHON: manu gəʦəɭən a qaɭiʦi ni ɖaiɖaiɭ, sa vaikan.
S.TRANSL: He pinched off the monkey’s penis, and went off with it.

S.FORM: manu vaik a sema vangaw ti gagang.
S.PHON: manu vaik a səma vaŋaw ti gagaŋ.
S.FORM: manu vaik a sema vangaw ti gagang.
S.PHON: manu vaik a səma vaŋaw ti gagaŋ.
S.TRANSL: The crab went up to the sky-light.

S.FORM: sipaɫeteku tua kinsa ni Tjukutjuku ti ḍaiḍail; kemakan anga ti ḍaiḍail.
S.PHON: sipaʎətəku tua kinsa ni cukucuku ti ɖaiɖaiɭ; kəmakan aŋa ti ɖaiɖaiɭ.
S.FORM: sipaljeteku tua kinsa ni Tjukutjuku ti draidrail; kemakan anga ti draidrail.
S.PHON: sipaʎətəku tua kinsa ni ʦukuʦuku ti ɖaiɖaiɭ; kəmakan aŋa ti ɖaiɖaiɭ.
S.TRANSL: Tjukutjuku set some food before the monkey, and the monkey was eating it.

S.FORM: tjanian a paka vangaw tua qalitsi pasa tua kinsa.
S.PHON: canian a paka vaŋaw tua qaɭiʦi pasa tua kinsa.
S.FORM: tjanian a paka vangaw tua qalici pasa tua kinsa.
S.PHON: ʦanian a paka vaŋaw tua qaɭiʦi pasa tua kinsa.
S.TRANSL: Through the sky-light was the way the crab dropped the penis into the food.

S.FORM: "iyaa, niaken a qalitsi tsu," aya ti ḍaiḍail, sa suɫuɫu-i, sa vaikan a tjumaq.
S.PHON: "ijaa, niakən a qaɭiʦi ʦu," aja ti ɖaiɖaiɭ, sa suʎuʎu-i, sa vaikan a cumaq.
S.FORM: "iyaa, niaken a qalici cu," aya ti draidrail, sa suljulju-i, sa vaikan a tjumaq.
S.PHON: "ijaa, niakən a qaɭiʦi ʦu," aja ti ɖaiɖaiɭ, sa suʎuʎu-i, sa vaikan a ʦumaq.
S.TRANSL: “Ugh! This is my penis,” said the monkey, and picked it out, and took it home.

S.FORM: "aku azua su sualapen a ku qalitsi?"
S.PHON: "aku azua su suaɭapən a ku qaɭiʦi"
S.FORM: "aku azua su sualapen a ku qalici?"
S.PHON: "aku azua su suaɭapən a ku qaɭiʦi"
S.TRANSL: “Why did you remove my penis?”

S.FORM: "nataqed aken sakamaya," aya ti gagang a kitevela.
S.PHON: "nataqəd akən sakamaja," aja ti gagaŋ a kitəvəɭa.
S.FORM: "nataqed aken sakamaya," aya ti gagang a kitevela.
S.PHON: "nataqəd akən sakamaja," aja ti gagaŋ a kitəvəɭa.
S.TRANSL: “I was asleep all the time,” said the crab to excuse himself.
That’s the end of the story.
